answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what activities or movements make the elbow pain worse or better?
answers.intake_a2: tennis makes it worse and rest makes this better
answers.intake_q3: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q4: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q5: Any prior injuries or repetitive strain to the elbow before this started?
answers.intake_q6: Any fever, chills, or other signs of infection since the elbow pain started?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain so far?
answers.intake_a7: I iced it the first day
answers.intake_q8: Is the pain located on the outer part of your elbow, inner part, or somewhere else?
answers.intake_q9: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your affected arm?
answers.intake_q10: Any prior history of arthritis or other joint problems?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outer part of her elbow. The pain worsens with playing tennis and lifting objects and improves with rest. She denies any swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the arm or hand. There is no history of prior injuries, repetitive strain, arthritis, or joint problems. She initially tried icing the elbow on the first day of symptoms. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by pain over the lateral elbow exacerbated by activity involving wrist extension and gripping. The absence of swelling, redness, or neurological symptoms supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen at recommended doses to reduce pain and inflammation. 
- Alternatives: Topical NSAID gels applied over the lateral elbow to minimize systemic side effects. 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid exceeding recommended doses of NSAIDs to prevent gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, renal, and hepatic adverse effects. Use caution in patients with underlying risk factors. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if there is worsening pain, development of swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever occur. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if atypical features develop.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
